title: Sensitivity of Polythiophene Planar Light-Emitting Diodes to Oxygen
abstract:
Communications
Sensitivity of Polythiophene Planar
Light-Emitting Diodes to Oxygen**
By Yvette Kaminorz,* Elisabeth Smela, Olle Inganäs, and
Ludwig Brehmer*
We have previously reported the microfabrication of
light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with electrodes underneath
the electroluminescent layer, which we called surface light-
emitting diodes (SLEDs).[1] This fabrication method is
compatible with conventional silicon processing, and light
is emitted from the upper surface of the diodes. In this pa-
per we report results from such structures with a rectangu-
lar bottom indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode separated by a
2500  thick insulating layer of silicon oxide from a comb-
shaped top Al electrode; the luminescent polymer was sol-
vent cast on top of the electrodes.
Understanding the role of oxygen in the degradation of
organic LEDs is of considerable importance. We were
therefore interested in exploring a light-emitting polymer's
reaction to oxygen exposure, both the nature of the re-
sponse and its kinetics. We exploited the SLED geometry
to perform these gas sensitivity studies, which would be dif-
ficult with conventional sandwich LED structures. (A simi-
lar polymer-on-top geometry has been used in our labora-
tory as the basis of an optical and chemical ªcameraº made
from an array of pixels.[2])
We
used
poly[3-(2¢,5¢-bis(1²,4²,7²-trioxaoctyl)phenyl)-
2,2¢-bithiophene] (EO-PT), which is a member of the poly-
thiophene family that has oligo(ethylene oxide) side chains
(ethylene oxide polythiophene). Its structure is shown in
Figure 1a. The side chains were chosen to enhance ionic
conduction in the polymer, and the polymer was designed
to be used in light-emitting electrochemical cells (LECs).[3]
The synthesis of the polymer was carried out by iron chlo-
ride coupling.[4] This type of polymer is more stable than
other polythiophenes, such as poly[3-(4-octylphenyl)-2,2¢-
bithiophene] (PTOPT), which was observed to degrade ra-
pidly in the presence of oxygen, when used in the present
device structure. The use of EO-PT allowed us to study the
quenching process quasi-independently from the degrada-
tion processes.
Device fabrication has been described in detail else-
where.[1] The structure used in this report is illustrated in
Figure 1b. It consists of a glass substrate with a rectangular
ITO electrode; on top of it there is a patterned comb-
shaped electrode separated from it by a (patterned) insulat-
ing silicon oxide (SiOx) layer. The two electrodes in this
configuration were therefore separated by a distance equal
to the oxide thickness, 2500 . The structures had either
two hundred 10 mm wide, 1500 mm long fingers separated
from each other by 10 mm, or one hundred 20 mm wide,
1500 mm long fingers separated from each other by 20 mm.
The I(V) and L(V) characteristics of EO-PT in nitrogen
and in air are compared in Figure 2. The light intensity, ap-
parent turn-on voltage (at which light is first measured by
the detector), and current are all affected by oxygen. At
the same voltage, the light output in nitrogen was larger by
an order of magnitude than in air. The turn-on voltage, 5±
10 V in nitrogen, was higher in air by ca. 5 V. Up to the
turn-on voltage, the current behaved the same way in both
nitrogen and air, but above this point the current was high-
er in the presence of oxygen. At low voltages, the current is
due to hole injection. The turn-on voltage is associated with
the start of electron injection. Note that the slope of the
I(V) curve increased abruptly with the on-set of light emis-
sion.
Adv. Mater. 1998, 10, No. 10

0935-9648/98/1007-0765 $ 17.50+.50/0
765
Communications
±
[*] Y. Kaminorz, Prof. L. Brehmer
Institute of Physics
Physics of Condensed Matter/Solid State Physics
University of Potsdam
Am Neuen Palais 10, D-14469 Potsdam (Germany)
Dr. E. Smela, Dr. O. Inganäs
Department of Applied Physics
Linköping University
S-58183 Linköping (Sweden)
[**] Y.K. and E.S. contributed equally to this work. The authors thank Dr.
Anita Spetz for her kind assistance and for the use of the gas handling
equipment.
Fig. 1. a) Chemical structure of EO-PT. b) Electrode configuration: a comb-
shaped top Al electrode is separated by a silicon oxide insulating layer with
the same pattern from the rectangular bottom ITO electrode. The conju-
gated polymer was solution cast over the electrodes to complete the device.
 Communications
766
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0935-9648/98/1007-0766 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 1998, 10, No. 10
Fig. 2. I(V) (open symbols) and L(V) (filled symbols) characteristics of an
EO-PT SLED in nitrogen (triangles) and air (circles).
The external quantum efficiency (QE) of the device
shown in Figure 2 in nitrogen is about 5 ´ 10±3 %. This is
the same order of magnitude as that of EO-PT sandwiched
between ITO and aluminum electrodes. For the light emis-
sion of EO-PT in oxygen, the QE is only 2 ´ 10±5 %.
In order to shed some light on the oxygen diffusion pro-
cess, to measure the kinetics of the response to oxygen and
the rate of recovery, the light intensity and current at a con-
stant voltage were monitored over time as the gas was
changed (Fig. 3). The device was flushed for 30 min under
N2 before these curves were recorded, in order to remove
the dissolved oxygen (although residual oxygen may still be
present[5]). When the device was first turned on, as shown
in the first part of Figure 3, the current and light intensity
increased. This ªwarm-upº period is typical for EO-PT de-
vices. When pure oxygen was substituted for nitrogen, the
light intensity dropped instantly almost to the 0 V baseline
and stayed low, whereas the current instantly increased.
The magnitude of the current increase was ca. 100 % dur-
ing both warm-up and steady-state operation when oxygen
was added. In contrast, when nitrogen was reapplied the
current dropped back to its previous value immediately,
but the light output recovered slowly, over several minutes.
The original light level was not attained again after the sec-
ond exposure to oxygen, most likely because of chemical
oxidation of the polymer.
Argon, hydrogen, and CO were also tested in this man-
ner. The response to argon was the same as to nitrogen, as
expected for an inert gas. No sensitivity of the light emis-
sion to either hydrogen or CO was observed.
To measure the electroluminescence (EL) spectra the
sample was mounted in the sample box and flushed with ni-
trogen for 30 min to remove most of the oxygen from the
polymer. The decay of the electroluminescence in air was
followed by taking several spectra one after the other with
an integration time of 1 s and an applied potential of 26 V.
The first two spectra were recorded in nitrogen. During ac-
quisition of the third spectrum the nitrogen was switched
off, and air diffused into the sample box. The first four and
the 26th spectra are shown in Figure 4, smoothed for
clarity. Notice that the intensity decayed immediately, and
in the fourth spectrum (less than 3 s in the presence of oxy-
gen) the light had decayed to its final level; the lumines-
cence peak was hardly detectable by the charge coupled
device (CCD) camera, although the dim light could still be
seen by eye. However, the shape of the spectrum did not
change during the decay. After this measurement, the ni-
trogen was switched on again, and the recovery of the light
intensity was monitored. The spectrum after ca. 5.5 min is
shown as a dashed line in Figure 4. (Note that this is not
the final recovered level; the intensity continued to in-
crease.) Again, the shape of the spectrum is the same and
the intensity is almost as high as at the beginning.
Fig. 4. Decay of electroluminescence when the LED is exposed to air. The
first two spectra were measured in nitrogen, the 3rd, 4th, and 26th in air.
The increase in electroluminescence when oxygen is removed is also
shownÐthe dashed line shows the spectrum after 5.5 min in nitrogen (26th
scan).
To help determine which quenching process was caused
by oxygen, we investigated the response of EO-PT to air dur-
ing excitation with UV light. The photoluminescence (PL)
was measured by excitation with a UV lamp fixed in position
so that the exposure intensity was the same for all measure-
ments. The sample was flushed for 50 min with nitrogen, and
Fig. 3. Current versus time, I(t), and light versus time, L(t), at 26 V forward
bias in nitrogen and pure oxygen. The gas flows were as follows: at t =
±30 min, N2 flow turned on; at t = 20 s, voltage turned on; at t = 400 s, O2; at
t = 600 s, N2; at t = 1900 s, O2; at t = 2100 s, N2. A 0 V baseline is shown in
the lower panel.

Sensitivity of Polythiophene Planar
Light-Emitting Diodes to Oxygen**
By Yvette Kaminorz,* Elisabeth Smela, Olle Inganäs, and
We have previously reported the microfabrication of
light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with electrodes underneath
the electroluminescent layer, which we called surface light-
emitting diodes (SLEDs).[1] This fabrication method is
compatible with conventional silicon processing, and light
is emitted from the upper surface of the diodes. In this pa-
per we report results from such structures with a rectangu-
lar bottom indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode separated by a
2500  thick insulating layer of silicon oxide from a comb-
shaped top Al electrode; the luminescent polymer was sol-
vent cast on top of the electrodes.
Understanding the role of oxygen in the degradation of
organic LEDs is of considerable importance. We were
therefore interested in exploring a light-emitting polymer's
reaction to oxygen exposure, both the nature of the re-
sponse and its kinetics. We exploited the SLED geometry
to perform these gas sensitivity studies, which would be dif-
ficult with conventional sandwich LED structures. (A simi-
lar polymer-on-top geometry has been used in our labora-
tory as the basis of an optical and chemical ªcameraº made
poly[3-(2¢,5¢-bis(1²,4²,7²-trioxaoctyl)phenyl)-
2,2¢-bithiophene] (EO-PT), which is a member of the poly-
thiophene family that has oligo(ethylene oxide) side chains
(ethylene oxide polythiophene). Its structure is shown in
Figure 1a. The side chains were chosen to enhance ionic
conduction in the polymer, and the polymer was designed
to be used in light-emitting electrochemical cells (LECs).[3]
The synthesis of the polymer was carried out by iron chlo-
ride coupling.[4] This type of polymer is more stable than

pidly in the presence of oxygen, when used in the present

ing silicon oxide (SiOx) layer. The two electrodes in this
configuration were therefore separated by a distance equal
to the oxide thickness, 2500 . The structures had either
two hundred 10 mm wide, 1500 mm long fingers separated
from each other by 10 mm, or one hundred 20 mm wide,
1500 mm long fingers separated from each other by 20 mm.
The I(V) and L(V) characteristics of EO-PT in nitrogen
and in air are compared in Figure 2. The light intensity, ap-
parent turn-on voltage (at which light is first measured by
the detector), and current are all affected by oxygen. At

10 V in nitrogen, was higher in air by ca. 5 V. Up to the

er in the presence of oxygen. At low voltages, the current is
due to hole injection. The turn-on voltage is associated with

Fig. 2. I(V) (open symbols) and L(V) (filled symbols) characteristics of an
EO-PT SLED in nitrogen (triangles) and air (circles).
The external quantum efficiency (QE) of the device
shown in Figure 2 in nitrogen is about 5 ´ 10±3 %. This is
the same order of magnitude as that of EO-PT sandwiched
between ITO and aluminum electrodes. For the light emis-
sion of EO-PT in oxygen, the QE is only 2 ´ 10±5 %.
In order to shed some light on the oxygen diffusion pro-
cess, to measure the kinetics of the response to oxygen and
the rate of recovery, the light intensity and current at a con-
stant voltage were monitored over time as the gas was
changed (Fig. 3). The device was flushed for 30 min under
N2 before these curves were recorded, in order to remove
the dissolved oxygen (although residual oxygen may still be
present[5]). When the device was first turned on, as shown
in the first part of Figure 3, the current and light intensity
increased. This ªwarm-upº period is typical for EO-PT de-
vices. When pure oxygen was substituted for nitrogen, the
light intensity dropped instantly almost to the 0 V baseline
and stayed low, whereas the current instantly increased.
The magnitude of the current increase was ca. 100 % dur-
ing both warm-up and steady-state operation when oxygen
was added. In contrast, when nitrogen was reapplied the
current dropped back to its previous value immediately,
but the light output recovered slowly, over several minutes.
The original light level was not attained again after the sec-
ond exposure to oxygen, most likely because of chemical
Argon, hydrogen, and CO were also tested in this man-
ner. The response to argon was the same as to nitrogen, as
expected for an inert gas. No sensitivity of the light emis-
sion to either hydrogen or CO was observed.
To measure the electroluminescence (EL) spectra the
sample was mounted in the sample box and flushed with ni-
trogen for 30 min to remove most of the oxygen from the
polymer. The decay of the electroluminescence in air was
followed by taking several spectra one after the other with
an integration time of 1 s and an applied potential of 26 V.
The first two spectra were recorded in nitrogen. During ac-
quisition of the third spectrum the nitrogen was switched
off, and air diffused into the sample box. The first four and
the 26th spectra are shown in Figure 4, smoothed for
clarity. Notice that the intensity decayed immediately, and
in the fourth spectrum (less than 3 s in the presence of oxy-
gen) the light had decayed to its final level; the lumines-
cence peak was hardly detectable by the charge coupled
device (CCD) camera, although the dim light could still be
seen by eye. However, the shape of the spectrum did not
change during the decay. After this measurement, the ni-
trogen was switched on again, and the recovery of the light
intensity was monitored. The spectrum after ca. 5.5 min is
shown as a dashed line in Figure 4. (Note that this is not
the final recovered level; the intensity continued to in-
crease.) Again, the shape of the spectrum is the same and
the intensity is almost as high as at the beginning.
Fig. 4. Decay of electroluminescence when the LED is exposed to air. The
first two spectra were measured in nitrogen, the 3rd, 4th, and 26th in air.
The increase in electroluminescence when oxygen is removed is also
shownÐthe dashed line shows the spectrum after 5.5 min in nitrogen (26th
To help determine which quenching process was caused
by oxygen, we investigated the response of EO-PT to air dur-
ing excitation with UV light. The photoluminescence (PL)
was measured by excitation with a UV lamp fixed in position
so that the exposure intensity was the same for all measure-
ments. The sample was flushed for 50 min with nitrogen, and
Fig. 3. Current versus time, I(t), and light versus time, L(t), at 26 V forward
bias in nitrogen and pure oxygen. The gas flows were as follows: at t =
±30 min, N2 flow turned on; at t = 20 s, voltage turned on; at t = 400 s, O2; at
t = 600 s, N2; at t = 1900 s, O2; at t = 2100 s, N2. A 0 V baseline is shown in
then three spectra were measured by turning on the UV
lamp just long enough to record the spectra, less than 5 s per
measurement. Between measurements the sample was kept
fixed and in nitrogen in the dark. The PL spectra had the
same shape as the EL spectra and did not vary significantly
over time under these conditions. The intensities of the PL
peak at 609 nm versus time are plotted in Figure 5. The ni-
trogen was then switched off, air diffused into the sample
box, and six spectra were recorded, one every 10 min. Notice
that the PL intensity did not change upon exposure to air,
unlike the EL, which dropped immediately. Furthermore, as
for EL, the shape of the spectra was unaffected. The sample
was flushed with nitrogen again and four spectra taken. The
intensity, as expected, remained constant.
Fig. 5. PL intensity measurements with alternating nitrogen and air expo-
sure. Each point in the graph represents one measurement of the PL spec-
trum. a) The UV lamp was switched on only for PL measurement and was
switched off between measurements. b) The UV lamp was kept on between
After that the sample, still under nitrogen, was exposed
to UV light by turning on the lamp continuously. The poly-
mer was unaffected by UV light when it was under nitro-
gen. However, the combination of UV light and oxygen
has been found by others[6±8] to photo-oxidize conjugated
polymers. Therefore, with the UV lamp kept on, the nitro-
gen was switched off. With air in the sample box, the PL in-
tensity gradually dropped. Reapplying nitrogen did not in-
crease the intensity, although it did halt the drop. In the
presence of air, the signal decayed to zero over a period of
hours. EO-PT was irreversible changed, probably due to
photo-oxidation.[6,7] This long-term aging in air also af-
fected the EL, as mentioned above.
We have observed that, in general, polythiophene-based
LEDs with Al electrodes stored in an oxygen-containing
atmosphere are not quickly degraded, but can be stored for
days without ill effect. Problems appear to be due to oxida-
tion of the Al electrodes, rather than the oxidation of the
polymer, because electrodes left in air longer than a few
days require cleaning, for example by dipping them in an
Al etchant, in order to function properly. It is only when
the devices are operated that significant oxygen-induced
degradation of EL performance occurs.
From the results presented above, it is apparent that the
effect of oxygen on light emission is rather complex. In Fig-
ures 2, 3, and 5 there are several effects to note. First, there
is the reduced quantum yield, typically down by a factor of
approximately 100. (There is also the apparent shift of the
turn-on in electroluminescence to higher voltages, but this
is probably because the light signal is below the noise level
of the detector, a consequence of the quenching.) Second,
there is the increase in current that is correlated with the
increase in light emission; until that point, the current with
and without oxygen is the same. That we observe no in-
crease in current below the turn-on voltage in the presence
of oxygen indicates that the enhancement of hole current is
minor. Third, it is apparent that the light quenching is im-
mediate, but that the recovery takes time. However,
whereas the current increase is also immediate, it drops
back to its previous value directly after nitrogen is reintro-
duced into the sample holder. This implies that oxygen
diffusion in the polymer is rapid. Finally, the PL is not af-
fected by oxygen alone. Thus, oxygen has two separate
effects: i) quenching of EL, which has a slow recovery; and
ii) an increase in electron current, which has a fast re-
An increase in conductivity upon oxygen exposure has
previously been observed by several groups using interdigi-
tated electrodes and field effect transistors (FETs)[5,9,10]
and was attributed to doping of the conjugated polymer by
oxygen, either by the formation of charge-transfer com-
plexes[10,11] or charged states formed via energy transfer.[12]
Measurements of photoconductivity show that it also rises
under an atmosphere of oxygen.[13] An increase in conduc-
tivity from weakly bound oxygen nevertheless probably oc-
On the other hand, it has been reported[14] that oxygen
decreases the work function of Al electrodes, which re-
duces the energy barrier and enhances the injection of elec-
trons. Oxygen may also cause a work function change in
ITO, and the barriers could be altered by a change in the
polymer's energy levels due to doping. Whether such ef-
fects may cause the excess current we see in the presence
of oxygen is not easy to refute or verify. However, the turn-
on voltage remained the same upon exposure to oxygen,
rather than decreasing as would be expected for a reduc-
tion in the barrier height for electron injection. Further,
the current below the turn-on voltage was identical with
and without oxygen, which indicates that hole-injection
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0935-9648/98/1007-0767 $ 17.50+.50/0
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0935-9648/98/1007-0768 $ 17.50+.50/0
from ITO was unaffected. The nature of charge injection
and transport in polythiophene LEDs has not been exten-
sively studied, and evidence for a Schottky barrier at the
Al/polythiophene contact has been presented.[15] Neither is
the charge transport in light-emitting electrochemical cells
well understood, but may include the formation of a doped
polymer. The ethylene oxide substituents of EO-PT are ex-
pected to increase the contribution of mobile dopants by
enhancing ion transport. This may allow the formation of a
doped region in the device, and thus affect the thickness of
the injection barriers at the electrodes. Our device may be
intermediate between an LED and an LEC.
What is the mechanism for the quenching? The funda-
mental light emission process must be unaffected because
the shapes of the EL and PL spectra are the same and are
unchanged when oxygen is added. Since the process is
largely reversible, it does not involve a chemical reaction
or other process that alters or damages the polymer. This is
unlike the case in the presence of UV light (see Fig. 5b), in
which the well-documented photochemical oxidation of the
polymer can occur.[6±8] (The ground state of oxygen is trip-
let, and oxygen interaction with excited organic species
can lead to the formation of a singlet oxygen, PL quench-
ing, and the degradation of the molecule.) The residual
quenching of EL in the device after exposure to oxygen
might be accounted for by the slow disappearance of bound
oxygen. Although the quenching process is mostly revers-
ible, nevertheless, after oxygen exposure the electrolumi-
nescence does not return entirely to its previous level. EO-
PT was chosen for these studies because of its high stability
in LEDs operated in air, but it is still subject to degradation
Both EL and PL have been assigned to the radiative re-
combination of singlet polaron excitons.[16] It was assumed
that oxygen deactivates excitons,[13] and in other polythio-
phenes significant PL quenching of 18 % by oxygen has
been observed.[10] In our samples the different response of
PL and EL to oxygen indicates that oxygen-induced
quenching does not occur primarily at the exciton level[2]
(even though the entire EO-PT film contributed to PL and
only the material near the edges of the top electrode con-
tributed to EL). We therefore conclude that oxygen can in-
fluence the charged states occurring prior to creation of the
exciton. Immediately upon injection of electrons and holes,
the formation of polarons is expected, and it is in this form
that the charged species exist until recombination into neu-
tral species (such as excitons).[16] In the simplest model,
oxygen, or some species created by the interaction of oxy-
gen with the polymer, may act to trap the electrons or
holes, dissociate excitons,[13,17] or non-radiatively recom-
bine the electron polarons and hole polarons. The presence
of oxygen-induced recombination centers in organic con-
ductors is well documented.[18] The recombination centers
may, for example, first capture an electron and subse-
quently a hole, thus annihilating the pair. The center is then
ready to repeat the process. The existence of loosely bound
oxygen that takes some time to diffuse out of the polymer
would explain the quenching of EL even after several min-
utes. The quenching process could therefore be catalytic,
with each oxygen molecule responsible for the destruction
of many exciton precursor species.
Further studies are clearly needed to understand these
processes, which would also be expected to occur in other
conjugated polymer LEDs. The effects are complex, but
the difference in time response can help decouple the
quenching from the current increase, and the difference in
EL and PL response can be used to probe the light genera-
In conclusion, for some basic scientific studies of light-
emitting diodes, having a geometry in which the organic
layer is accessible is advantageous. One example has been
presented in this paper, which looked at the effect of oxy-
gen exposure. The polymer-on-top configuration allows the
gases to diffuse quickly into the film during device opera-
tion and also enables PL measurements on the same de-
vice. Oxygen quenches electroluminescence and increases
the electron current in EO-PT. Removal of oxygen causes
the electroluminescence to recover and the current to fall
again, but with different time constants. Thus, the quench-
ing is not due to degradation. Photoluminescence is insensi-
tive to oxygen alone, so excitons are not directly involved
in the quenching mechanism. The latter two findings were
unexpected but may also apply to other conjugated poly-
The fabrication process was performed as follows: The ITO (13 W/&)
was patterned photolithographically (the etchant was a mixture of nitric acid
(65 %), hydrochloric acid (fuming), and water, 4:48:48 by volume). An insu-
lating layer of 2500  silicon oxide was deposited over the bottom electrode
by chemical vapor deposition. This was followed by the thermal evaporation
of 1000  aluminum for the top electrode, which was also patterned photo-
lithographically using a second mask. The silicon oxide was then patterned
using the same mask. To complete the device, the EO-PT was solvent cast
over the electrodes from a 5 mg/mL chloroform solution to a thickness of
700±1000 nm. The base electrode structure could be reused if the polymer
was removed, for example, by rinsing with chloroform. Electrode structures
that had been left in air for more than a few days required cleaning in either
cold SC1 solution (ammonia (25 %), hydrogen peroxide (30 %), and water,
1:1:5 by volume) for 10±20 s or an aluminum etchant, otherwise they did not
For current±voltage I(V), luminance±voltage L(V), current±time I(t), and
luminance±time L(t) measurements in different atmospheres, the sample
was mounted in a box that included a silicon photodetector, a gas inlet port
with a tube connection to different gas sources, and two probes to connect
the device electrically to the power supply. For measuring the electrolumi-
nescence spectra, a sample box with a window above the sample was used.
To measure the light intensity in different atmospheres, the tube connected
through the gas inlet port was changed. The gas pressure was 0.5 to 1 bar
and the purities were: nitrogen >99.9997 %; oxygen, argon, hydrogen, and
CO 99.95 % or better. The sample box was continuously supplied with gas,
except during air exposure, for which the gas supply was simply turned off
and air allowed to diffuse into the sample box.
The I(V) and L(V) characteristics of the devices were measured by apply-
ing a voltage ramp in small steps. The I(t) and L(t) characteristics were mea-
sured by applying a constant voltage (using a Keithley programmable elec-
trometer 617) and simultaneously reading both current (Keithley program-
mable electrometer 617) and light output (Hamamatsu silicon photodiode
S1337-1010BR, Keithley autoranging picoammeter 485). For calculating ex-
ternal quantum efficiency (QE), the spectral response and area of the detec-
tor were taken into account and a Lambertian distribution of the light was
For PL measurements, a UV lamp, peaking at 360 nm, was used as the
excitation source. For recording the PL and EL spectra the emitted light
was transmitted by an optical fiber to an InstaSpec CCD detector used with
an MS257 monochromator from Oriel Instruments.
Final version: February 27, 1998
[1] Y. Kaminorz, E. Smela, O. Inganäs, L. Brehmer, Semicond. Sci. Tech-
[2] D. Pede, E. Smela, T. Johansson, M. Johanssen, O. Inganäs, Adv. Ma-
[3] Q. Pei, G. Yu, C. Zhang, Y. Yang, A. J. Heeger, Science 1995, 269,
[4] W. Mammo, M. Andersson, O. Inganäs, in preparation.
[5] W. Väterlein, B. Ziegler, W. Gebauer, H. Neureiter, M. Stoldt, M. S.
Weaver, P. Bäuerle, M. Sokolowski, D. D. C. Bradley, E. Umbach,

Ultrathin Self-Assembled Layers at the ITO
Interface to Control Charge Injection and
Electroluminescence Efficiency in Polymer
By Peter K. H. Ho, Magnus Granström,
Richard H. Friend, and Neil C. Greenham*
Polymer light-emitting diodes (LEDs) have recently at-
tracted considerable attention[1,2] because of their potential
application in flat-panel displays. It is now appreciated that
the electrode/polymer interfaces play important, though
still poorly understood, roles in determining the operating
characteristics and stability of these devices. In this com-
munication, we demonstrate a new strategy to modify these
characteristics by interposing a well-defined, continuous
and ultrathin polymer layer (on the order of 10 ) at the
interface between the indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode
and the emissive polymer (Fig. 1a). We formed these inter-
layers using the layer-by-layer polyelectrolyte self-assembly
technique.[3] To illustrate the versatility of this approach,
we fabricated separate devices with a conducting polymer
(CP) interlayer of self-doped polyaniline, a semiconducting
polymer (SP) interlayer containing oligo-p-phenylenevinyl-
ene sequences, or an insulating polymer (IP) interlayer of
saturated main chain polymers. We show that a 10±20  in-
terlayer significantly alters charge injection, enhances the
device electroluminescence efficiency, and improves the
ITO/polymer interface quality, without causing a significant
increase in the drive voltage.
Charge injection in polymer LEDs can be modified in
several ways. Hole-transport layers,[4] electron-transport
layers,[5] or conducting-polymer layers[6±8] with thicknesses
of several hundred angstroms have been investigated. The
effect of thin insulating barriers has also been investi-
gated,[9±11] although these studies concentrated on interfa-
cial layers formed at the cathode interface. In the present
work, we employed polymer self-assembly as a tool to con-
struct interfacial layers at the ITO interface. First described
by Decher et al. in 1992,[3] polymer self-assembly provides
a versatile route to build up nanostructured polymer films
by sequential adsorption of polymer chains from solution.
The adsorption can be directed by electrostatic interac-
tions, or by other specific interactions such as hydrogen
bonding. By repeated adsorption of successive layers, it is
possible to construct the entire emissive layer in polymer
LEDs in this way.[11±14] This technique enables the composi-
tion to be varied within the device, allowing layers with a
particular functionality to be placed next to one of the elec-
trodes.[11,14] In the present work, however, we focus on
using the self-assembly technique to form a variety of ultra-
thin interfacial layers before depositing the emissive layer
by conventional spin-coating. This approach is less time-
consuming and more readily adapted to existing processing
The ITO surface was first derivatized to form a positively
+ groups by silylation. These substrates
were then alternately immersed in a polyanion and then a
polycation solution. This assembled a conformal interfacial
layer in the form of an alternating bilayered polyelectrolyte
stack. The polymer structures used in the CP-n, SP-n, and
IP-n interlayers, where n denotes the number of bilayers as-
sembled, are shown in Figure 1b. One, two, four, and ten
bilayers of each type (spanning 5±150  in total thickness)
were deposited. The thickness was determined by ellipsom-
etry, taking into consideration the ITO surface roughness.
poly(2-methoxy-5-(2¢-ethylhexyloxy)-p-phenyl-
enevinylene) (MEH-PPV) film was then spin-coated onto
these polyelectrolyte layers, and the partially completed
devices annealed in vacuum. Calcium contacts, followed by
